This is something I considered for a while, but ultimately decided against implementing for a couple of reasons: 

1) it would require significant complication of input (how would the user provide this information? in a table?) the program already has so many command-line arguments that I'm weary of adding more

2) it would require a number of internal changes to many modules to handle this information, i.e. a signifiant coding-time investment when there are other, arguably more important changes that could be made first

3) most importantly, it's relatively easy for the user to work-around this issue. Some possible solutions are:

- first run dRep on each sample, take the resulting "dereplicated genomes" from each sample, and then fun dRep again on all of them

- using the data tables "Sdb" and "Cdb", this should be relatively easy for the user to implement themself in R or excel. This way they can parse their genome names to figure out where it came from using whatever method they came up with, under an arbitrary number of groupings. Sdb contains the score of each genome, and Cdb lists each genome's secondary cluster.

Thank you for the suggestion, and please let me know if you have any others! :)

I can see addressing this issue in a couple of different ways:

## One-Time Fuzzing ##

Run the fuzzer against the library, identify failure modes, file bugs, write tests, and fix the bugs.

## Integrated Fuzzing Tests ##

We could integrate the fuzzing into the test suite such that tests are repeatedly run against random inputs (and could fail at some point in the future).

## Other considerations ##

Some other things to consider:

1. Do we test the client, the server, or both?
2. Do we test the protocol, the command-line inputs, or both?
3. Which configuration is used on the process (encoding/decoding config)? To some extent, the system is expected to fail on some inputs under certain configurations.

Overall, I feel like the value of this fuzzing is minimal, as it will under most circumstances only find bugs that will never be encountered. On the other hand, it might expose security vulnerabilities, which should be addressed. In the example above, I believe the behavior of the library is reasonable (raising a traceback when invalid arguments are passed on the command line) and not worth fixing.

Given these considerations, feel free to respond or put together a pull request if you believe the value justifies the effort.

Kent Watsen <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> I don't know what "canonical order" means.

The error message says:

 error: keyword "if-feature" not in canonical order, (See RFC 6020,
 Section 12

RFC 6020, Section 12 says:

   In YANG, almost all statements are unordered.  The ABNF grammar
   [RFC5234] defines the canonical order.  To improve module
   readability, it is RECOMMENDED that clauses be entered in this order.


So you probably have some other statement before if-feature.

To add user1_dn and user2_dn to group_dn:
```
conn.modify(group_dn, {'member': (MODIFY_ADD, [user_dn, user2_dn])}
```

To remove user1_dn and user2_dn from group_dn:
```
conn.modify(group_dn, {'member': (MODIFY_DELETE, [user_dn, user2_dn])}
```

To get all group members:
```
connection.search(group, '(objectclass=*)', BASE, attributes=['member'])
```

``MODIFY_ADD``, ``MODIFY_DELETE`` and ``BASE`` must be imported from the ``ldap3`` namespace.

Also in version 2.2 I have included two methods in the extend namespace:
```
conn.extend.microsoft.add_members_to_groups([user1, user2], [group1, group2])
conn.extend.microsoft.remove_members_from_groups([user1, user2], [group1, group2])
```

to add and remove users from groups.

        twt = re.sub(r'(?i)\b((?:https?://|www\d{0,3}[.]|[a-z0-9.\-]+[.][a-z]{2,4}/)(?:[^\s()<>]+|\(([^\s()<>]+|(\([^\s()<>]+\)))*\))+(?:\(([^\s()<>]+|(\([^\s()<>]+\)))*\)|[^\s`!()\[\]{};:\'".,<>?«»“”‘’]))', '', tt)

        #removing any other punctuation or unwanted character
        tw=re.sub(r"[.,;:$&*|?'-]",'',twt)
        

        #removing stopwords
        stop_words = set(stopwords.words('english'))
        word_tokens = word_tokenize(tw)
        filtered_sentence = [w.lower() for w in word_tokens if not w in stop_words]
        filtered_sentence = []
        for w in word_tokens:
            if w not in stop_words:
                filtered_sentence.append(w)

        #changes the tweet back into a string

yes, I agree that capnpy should allow to assign unicode objects to Text fields.

What to do for reading is an open question. Converting to unicode is prohibitively expensive: that's why I decided *not* to do it by default (also because utf8-encoded string is a perfectly reasonable representation for most tasks).

It would be nice to make the implicit utf8-to-unicode conversion optional. I see two main possibilities:
1) add a compiler option to enable the implicit conversion (which is disabled by default); and possibly use capnproto annotations to forcibly enable/disable the implicit conversion on a per-Struct/per-field basis

2) always add a ``u_*`` or ``*_u`` accessor for each Text field, which does the conversion for you. So for example, if you have a Text ``foo`` field, doing ``obj.foo`` gives you the utf8 ``str`` object, while doing ``obj.u_foo`` gives you the ``unicode`` object

Note that PyPy will soon switch to utf8 for its internal representation of unicode objects, which means that the conversion will be a no-op. But of course this doesn't change anything for CPython, unless we want to develop a custom str-like object which does that.

@craffel I've finished implementing the AOR metric, and so I've compared it to some MIREX results: https://gist.github.com/justinsalamon/b2a837a889a61a69b76a42a4ff38d889

In the first plot you can see the `mir_eval` results are pretty different from the MIREX results. The reason is that this metric is highly dependent on the note matching, and since we use a different matching algorithm, the matching is different and consequently the AOR too. In the second plot I've plotted the scores we'd get if `mir_eval` used the same greedy note matching as in MIREX, and you can see the scores now match just fine.

The reason AOR is so sensitive to note matching is because it's the average (over all matched notes) of the ratio between the overlapping segment of two matched notes and the maximum time duration spanned by the two notes. So for example, lets say greedy matching matches ref note A to est note B with start/end times [1, 3] [0.8, 2.8] respectively. Their overlap ratio will be (2.8 - 1)/(3 - 0.8) = 1.8/2.2 = 0.82. Now, let's say graph-based note matching instead matches ref note A to est note C with start/end times [1.2, 2]. Now the overlap ratio will be (2 - 1.2)/(3 - 1) = 0.8/2 = 0.4, a dramatic drop from 0.82.

All this to say, I'm very confident my implementation of the metric is correct, it's just going to be different compared to MIREX due to the different note matching algorithm used. To get a rough notion of how the metric changes I computed the differences for all algorithms evaluated on the Su dataset in 2015, displayed in the third plot of the notebook. You can see the change in score actually depends more on the specific track being evaluated and less on the algorithm (which is not surprising). For this dataset, changes range from an increase of 0.02 to a drop of almost -0.2. The last (box)plot in the notebook gives a good feel for the spread of differences. The median difference is about -0.02. Results are likely to vary for other datasets.

This is a bigger problem than I thought which requires a consistent refactoring. There are different C functions using poor undocumented OSX APIs such as `proc_pidinfo` not returning a meaningful error in case of failure and as such we guess what happened from python by using `pid_exists()`, or even from C itself via `os.kill`.

The `wrap_exception` decorator implements this logic (e.g. to guess zombies) but there are some functions such as `cext.proc_kinfo_oneshot` which are not "poor" (they return the real OSError from the original C call) so don't need such a guessing. `status()` method is one of these. To push this even further, in case of zombie `proc_kinfo_oneshot` succeeds (and correctly reports status == ZOMBIE) whereas `proc_pidtaskinfo_oneshot` fails with ESRCH. So the whole logic needs to be carefully checked and possibly reorganized (not easy). I'm still not sure but what we may wanna do is delegate the whole "guessing" logic to Python, not to C, and from C raise `RuntimeError` or a custom exception (better). From Python, that'll men we're supposed to guess whether to raise `NoSuchProcess`, `AccessDenied` or `ZombieProcess`.

This is a late comment, but this broke my system. I recently upgraded my server to the next Ubuntu LTS and with it came the new Unoconv.

My system makes a tempfile with a random name and without extension, then called the `unoconv` binary and supplied the path to the `--output` parameter. Then my system went looking for this file, which wasn't there because it was looking for `/tmp/tempfile_TQC4Nd` and unoconv wrote to `/tmp/tempfile_TQC4Nd.csv`.

I worked around this by doing the following:
`(unoconv -vvvvvvvvvvvvvvvv --format csv --stdout infile.xls > /tmp/tempfile_TQC4Nd) 2>&1`

I'm using a subshell here so I can redirect the stderr messages to the stdout without it ending up in the converted file (and log the debug messages).

For the record, this is not a complaint (Unoconv has made my life so much easier when dealing with awful Office file formats), I just wanted to say that sometimes changes that seem low impact can indeed break other peoples systems. And if there is someone else who ran into this problem, they can use my solution.

I know that write operations can fail, but in this case, it is a string. If overwriting the existing file fails (after truncating, I imagine this is the disaster you have in mind), then writing to a temporary file wouldn't help. Have you a scenario for that?

You don't actually need to understand threefry to use threefry. This is for all of the basicRNGs since they are all forced to expose the same interface via the brng type. This type contains next_uint64, next_uint32 and next_double functions as well as the opaque basicRNG-dependent state.

```
cimport cython
from cpython.pycapsule cimport PyCapsule_GetPointer
from randomgen.common cimport brng_t

def myf(r):
    cdef brng_t *rng
    cdef uint64_t i
    cdef const char *name = 'BasicRNG'
    rng = <brng_t *> PyCapsule_GetPointer(r.capsule, name)
    i = rng.next_uint64(rng.state)

    return i
```

1. storage adapters: [sql-storage-adapter](https://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/storage/index.html#sql-storage-adapter)
2. logic adapter:  [Best Match Adapter](https://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/logic/index.html#best-match-adapter)
3. response selection: [get-first-response](https://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/logic/response_selection.html#chatterbot.response_selection.get_first_response)

You are always getting first statement due to your response selection method is get first response from your trained object. 

You may try to use [get_most_frequent_response](https://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/logic/response_selection.html#chatterbot.response_selection.get_most_frequent_response) or [get_random_response](https://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/logic/response_selection.html#chatterbot.response_selection.get_random_response) to resolve this issue.

Ratelimits are calculated on staggered, _fixed_ windows, i.e., for a given set of group/path/methods/key-value/period, ratelimit will calculate some value that will be the same every time it's calculated for `period` seconds (it uses the value to stagger them so, e.g. not all hour ratelimits expire at the top of the hour).

So, imagine the limit is `1/min`, the second attempt (`&`) will be ratelimited, but the 3rd won't be, even though it's within 1 minute of the first:

```
period:       |             |
attempts:            ^  &    ^
```

The reason for this is that if you reset the period every time (what ratelimit _used_ to do) then you can end up blocking even good actors basically forever (e.g. they try every 59 seconds, continually pushing the limit out and never being allowed).

So what's probably happening is that every once in a while, you cross the boundary during that loop. The odds are bad—and your flakiness rate is low—but it's entirely possible.

You could probably simplify this by mocking `ratelimit.utils.is_ratelimited` to always return `True` during this test, instead of trying to actually trip the limit. Alternatively, set the number of attempts to twice the limit, to make sure you hit it even if you cross the boundary (but you'll have to drop some of the assertions within the loop).

Thanks for the interest and the submission. I looked over the patch. It's a good idea and it certainly looks functional, but there's a couple reasons I wouldn't include this as is. 

The biggest one is that one of the goals of the module is to provide a uniform *cross-platform* audio interface. I am very hesitant to introduce any feature that is not fully cross-platform and your patch only addresses the Linux/ALSA approach to device selection. It may be possible to do essentially the same thing for both Windows and OSX but it would take digging back into their API's and coming up with a lowest-common-denominator solution. 

Another issue is that of usability with respect to audio device enumeration. If there's a way to specify the audio device being used, then there needs to be a way to query for all available devices in a *simple*, Pythonic way. This would need to be implemented as well. 

If all those goals could be met, then I'd be willing to move forward with the idea. Unfortunately, as much as I'd like to dive into all this, I have very little time to spare these days so I can't at the moment. If you'd like to take that on then I'd be happy to correspond and strategize further.

If you are on Django < 1.7, you need South, and we also need to specify a South version - >= 1.0 IIRC. If you use an older version, then you'll get errors. So, the reality is:

* If you are on Django < 1.7, you must use South and it must be >= 1.0
* If you are on Django > 1.7 then you don't need South

The problem is that setup.py has no way of specifying either of these. You can't do a "import django", because it might not be installed before django-paypal. In theory using "setup_requires" instead of "install_requires" caters for this, but "setup_requires" does not work.

At some point, we should probably assume that most people have migrated to Django 1.7, and simply document the requirement on South > 1.0 for Django < 1.7, and leave it out of the setup.py script altogether.

- Add an additional configuration key for `LDAP_ADD_SERVER`, defaults to `True`, and if set to `False`, `self.add_server` is not called from within `init_config`.  This will allow users to specify their own server later.
- Modify `add_server` to accept a new argument `tls_ctx=None` (to not break existing usages), which if defined, will be used as this server's TLS context. If tls_ctx is specified and use_ssl is False, then we should raise a `ValueError` since this combination doesn't make sense.

After having another think about JWT and security, I'm having some questions you may be able to help with or at least give me your opinion. The mean reason I'm choosing token-based authentication with JWT is to avoid all the overload of having stateful sessions and having to store all the details in the DB.

Now, if security is an issue for you, then you need to think about a mechanism to invalidate tokens if the situation requires it (token compromise, logout, user changing passwords, etc.). For this, most people recommend token revocation, for which you need to then keep the tokens stored in the server, and check them every time, etc., But then, why don't stick to good old cookies if you end up doing kind of the same thing?!

May it be the case that building on the concept of 'adding user context' to the tokens helps deal with this issue in a more effective way that avoids having to add statefulness to something that is meant to be stateless?

While django 1.8 show a warning when `django.utils.importlib.import_module` is used, we should not even tried to import `django.utils.importlib` in django 1.8 because `django.utils.importlib` exists for instance. Note that if you change the order of import, it would fix the problem but it might cause side-effects (while I'm not sure if `django.utils.importlib.import_module` of django 1.3 or so on is compatible with `importlib.import_module`) thus I prefer `if django.VERSION < (1, 8)` more.

Thanks for the report! I missed this as the underlying decorator / context manager code was adapted from django's `override_settings` and that always requires some sort of args. The underlying problem was that, without the parenthesis, the function to be wrapped was being passed into `__init__` as the first arg. The first arg supplied (if any) should have been the storage class / object instead.

Resolved by essentially requiring that all supplied arguments (if any) must be kwargs so that we don't accidentally pass the function to be wrapped to the init methods. You may have to update your code if you have been using positional arguments but this is much safer.

Mkdocs-material does not seem to support the class name `highlight` fully (the default for `pymdownx.highlight`).  It may support it for normal blocks, but numbered blocks also have some special styling under the class `highlighttable` which mkdocs-material has no styling for, but it does style `codehilitetable`.  You can use the class name `codehilite` and everything will work.  This is mainly because most people use `CodeHilite` extension and not `pymdownx.highlight` ( I don't, so I have to force the class name `codehilite`).  It's possible you can request that mkdocs-material duplicates styling under `codehilitetable` for `highlighttable`.

I did some digging and this is indeed a problem caused by virtualenv’s design. virtualenv uses [`site.py`](https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/blob/6f424ca/virtualenv_embedded/site.py) to hook into Python’s startup sequence and perform customisation.

Python is hard-wired to detect `../lib/pythonX.Y/site.py` when it starts up. If a `site.py` does not exist on the call side (i.e. in the virtual environment), it automatically uses the default `site.py` instead. This is how the interpreter is designed. So unfortunately there is no way to fix this, even in virtualenv.

@dan-blanchard I agree -- we actually had two variants here, one where it was specified as a string:

```python
    word_count_bolt = WordCountBolt.spec(
            input=WordSpout) #=> expands to ("word_spout", "default")
    # but, the "input" line could be:
            input="word_spout" #=> expands to ("word_spout", "default")
            input=("word_spout", "default")) #=> no sugar
```

So, the idea here was that if you point to the class, the assumption is there is only one instance of that class in your topology. (Otherwise, Topology is ill-defined; error thrown.) In the second variant, the idea is that there must be a field named in the Topology class which will resolve to that component -- and, the "default" stream is implied. The final variant, the `tuple` form, specifies both a component ID (via Python field name) and a stream ID (in this case, "default").

Makes sense?

First, here are the comments:

```
# Error imports.  Note that we may want to move these out of the root
# namespace in the future and you should prefer to access them via
# the fully qualified `stripe.error` module.

# DEPRECATED: These imports will be moved out of the root stripe namespace
# in version 2.0
```
Second, there are two related issues here.

1) Relative imports to files with simple names (like error.py) can break, especially when a module is moved.
2) Absolute imports prevent a module from being anywhere but where the original author intended it to be. This causes several problems:
a) Unnecessary clutter at the root of servers
b) Inability to work in special environments that may impose requirements on where libraries can be installed

Personally, I think PEP 8 is off the mark in recommending absolute imports. While they solve problems caused by implicit relative imports, they are clearly inferior to explicit relative imports in the context of libraries installed as the Stripe library is installed. You should also read PEP 328.

While (2b) doesn't apply for the Stripe library in the Google App Engine flexible environment, it could certainly apply in other situations. A related issue is libraries that expect to be installed via an installer, rather than having a directory placed at a certain location. Such installers cannot run on GAE.

Finally, what I wrote was wrong. You want an _explicit_ relative import, not an implicit one. They were included as of Python 2.5. What you want is:
```
from .resource import ...
```
or
```
from . import ...
```

Switching to explicit relative imports has no disadvantages, is very clear, and has the advantage of allowing Stripe to be installed anywhere.

Ok, here's the relevant diff, left is 0.12, right is 47af70a: The difference is that in the old version, the output pixel gets labeled at the time that the pixel is pushed onto the heap, while in the new version, it gets labeled at the time that it's *popped* from the heap. When compactness is not a factor, the first version is correct, because the labelling is determined solely by which label gets there first. (If they get there at the same time, then it's arbitrary and you might as well give it to the label that pushes first.) But when compactness *is* a factor, labelling at push-time is a bug, because a different neighbor could get there "later" but actually be "closer" when compactness is factored in. I'm going to isolate the commit where I changed this (I remember needing to do this), and then I think it can probably be fixed by adding an extra branch in the loop. Alternatively the most efficient route might be to duplicate most of the code and have two different loops, one for compactness and one for classical watershed.

I'm afraid this change won't work as is, because it breaks the Python interface: redis-py returns a boolean from `.exists` and we need to preserve that.

I think you've uncovered the tip of an iceberg: redis-py does further interpretation on the value returned from the raw redis protocol, using a table. In fakeredis, these conversions are built directly into the methods, so when they're called by Lua, Lua gets the converted value instead of the value in the raw redis protocol. You'll almost certainly be able to find other cases e.g. INCRBYFLOAT is probably going to give Lua a float when in real redis it would get a string.

Fixing this consistently will probably require architectural changes, e.g. to split these functions into two parts (one that emulates the raw redis protocol and a wrapper that emulates the redis-py type conversions).

This is currently not supported, however the HDF5 standard does make this possible using [chunking](https://support.hdfgroup.org/HDF5/Tutor/extend.html). That is, an array is stored as separate chunks, so when extending the array, the memory does not have to be contiguous in the file (because that would require a full re-allocation of the file). I know the backend PyTables can set chunking, so I think deepdish could support this. This feature request has come up before and I think it would be really neat, so I will consider this as high priority.

If I don't get to this soon, anyone is welcome to propose a PR.

There are some design decisions that need to be made first. The chunking size needs to be known the first time you save the file. Either deepdish sets it to something appropriate, or the user explicitly specifies a chunk size. What is the API for extending an array? Perhaps a separate call altogether, like `dd.io.extend('test.h5', '/foo', x, axis=0)`?

Ah, yes. Py3 does support a timeout there, but Py2 doesn't. The subprocess module in Py2 is pretty poor overall, but much better in Py3. But you should really never call wait unless you want to block. That's why it's called "wait". Use poll method if you don't want to block. If the main thread is blocking forever then something isn't right with the process management... You might have to read from it first to drain the pipe that's it's blocked on. You're also not getting the exit status, so the main thread won't know if the subprocess terminated normally or not. But given that this change is just working around another issue I'll just say go with it. --- *Comments from [Reviewable](https://reviewable.io:443/reviews/google/mobly/332)*

### 1. Use a synchronous transport e.g.

```python
from raven.transport.http import HTTPTransport
app.config['SENTRY_TRANSPORT'] = HTTPTransport
```

### 2. Employ a deferred send in an AppEngin'y way

For example, the  [raven-appengine](https://github.com/danielchatfield/raven-appengine) package.

##  Fix?
Obviously it would be worthwhile for this to appear in the docs for Raven[Flask], or for it to complain about it's running on AppEngine and the transport is asynchronous.

Pivoting
------
[Pivot functionality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pivot_table) comes from early spreadsheet applications, Lotus Improv and Excel. In a nutshell, the functionality involves designating a "rows" column, a "columns" column, and a "data" column, plus an aggregator function. Then for each distinct (rows, columns) combination, we compute aggregator(data). Thus, pivoting involves:
- selecting columns `R`, `C` and `V` from the target frame;
- grouping the target frame by `(R, C)`;
- for each group computing `agg(V)`;
- converting the produced frame into "wide" format with values of `C` becoming the columns.

Thus, I can think of 2 ways how to express this in `datatable` syntax:

1. Emphasizing the way how people usually think about pivot tables 
    ```
    DT.pivot[f.R, f.C, agg(f.V)]
    ```
    This draws upon the traditional form `DT[i, j]` where `i` corresponds to rows, and `j` to columns.

2. Emphasizing what pivoting actually **is**:
    ```
    DT.pivot[:, agg(f.V), by(f.R, f.C)]
    ```
    This requires some more cognitive processing, but is more flexible: one can add a rows filter, and use extra parameters of the `by` function to fine-tune grouping. 

Note: same function is called `dcast()` in `data.table`, we may choose to use that verb instead.

> How will one access the complex array at the C level?

In the C++ layer, it's an array of `std::complex<double>`.  That's just two doubles sequentially in memory: real then imag.  That's the same way numpy stores things for an array of `np.complex128`.  It's also the way FFTW expects things for their complex type `fftw_complex`.

[Here](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/%23799/pysrc/NumpyHelper.h#L202) is the line that casts the C++ memory into a complex numpy array.  T = `std::complex<double>`, and `getCode()` will return `NPY_COMPLEX128`.  The [line](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/%23799/pysrc/NumpyHelper.h#L122) to go back the other way from numpy data to `std::complex<double>*` is even simpler, since it's just a `reinterpret_cast`.

The `calculate_output` function is opt-in: by default it calls `calculate`.

Add `calculate_output = calculate_output_divide` to the attributes of `tehr`:

```python
class tehr(Variable):
    column = FloatCol
    entity_class = Individus
    label = u"Taxe exceptionnelle de solidarité sur les très hautes rémunérations"
    url = u"http://vosdroits.service-public.fr/professionnels-entreprises/F32096.xhtml"
    calculate_output = calculate_output_divide

    def function(self, simulation, period):
        period = period.start.period(u'year').offset('first-of')
        salaire_de_base = simulation.calculate_add('salaire_de_base', period)  # TODO: check base
        law = simulation.legislation_at(period.start)

        bar = law.cotsoc.tehr
        return period, -bar.calc(salaire_de_base)
```

Now the test passes.

Background info: we know that `tehr` is a numeric value which can be divided or multiplied if the period (year) is reduced or extended. That's not the case for every numeric values, think of `taille_entreprise` for example! This should not be an automatic feature.

Just to make it clear for people reading that issue later on:
 - the issue is indeed in `urllib3` and has been fixed with https://github.com/shazow/urllib3/pull/719
 - BUT upgrading `urllib3` **won't solve** the issue because `requests` embed it's own version of `urllib3`; so the API client won't use that updated version
 - the good thing is that `requests` upgraded to `urllib3 1.13` in their `2.9.x` release

**tldr;**

```
pip install requests[security]==2.9.1
```

This is probably two separate issues (that are somewhat at odds with each other)

1. it is not uncommon for users to unintentionally use non-ascii characters by copy/pasting something that had `--` converted to an emdash or a space converted to some weird unicode space, and in these cases, users would benefit from a clearer error message
2. there are cases in which a user has a legitimate need to pass a non-ascii character (e.g. to use pytest's `-k` to select a test whose name contains an `á`), and they are prevented from doing so. I don't see a trivial solution to solve this thoroughly, since we don't have a way of specifying the encoding and we could theoretically have different args with different encodings.

(2) is at odds with (1) since solving (2) would probably generate an even more obtuse error message like `Unknown goals: —`. At least the current error message points the user in the direction of encodings.

@gaow @stephens999 @jean997 I agree that groups is the best way to resolve this. Here's my implementation of Matthew's suggestion:

```yaml
simulate: R(dat <- rnorm(n = 1000,mean = 0,sd = 1) +
                   rbinom(n = 1000,size = 1,prob = 0.05) *
                   rexp(n = 1000,rate = 3))
    $dat: dat

unbiased_mean: R(m <- mean(x))
    x: $dat
    $est_mean: m

huber_mean: R(library(MASS); m <- huber(x,k = k)$mu)
    x: $dat
    k: 0.5, 1.5, 5
    $est_mean: m

sq_error: R(sq_error <- est^2)
    $sq_err: sq_error
    est: $est_mean

DSC:
    define: mean: unbiased_mean, huber_mean
    run: simulate * mean * sq_error
    replicate: 3
```

  targets = c("mean.est_mean","huber_mean.k","sq_error.sq_err"))

I ended up writing this fleet service which exposes the docker socket over TCP and connect to it from the drone app. It assumes that the port is not publicly accessible and will probably break when Deis starts preventing Deis app from connecting to the host.

```
[Unit]
Description=Docker reverse proxy
Requires=docker.socket
After=docker.socket

# Make sure the port 2376 is not accessible from outside your
# internal network. The connection to docker is not authenticated.
[Service]
ExecStartPre=/bin/sh -c "docker history olalond3/docker-reverse-proxy >/dev/null || docker pull olalond3/docker-reverse-proxy"
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c "\
  docker run \
    --name docker-reverse-proxy \
    -h %H \
    -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
    -p 2376:2375 \
    olalond3/docker-reverse-proxy; \
"

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

[X-Fleet]
Global=true%
```

```
FROM gliderlabs/alpine

RUN apk add --update-cache \
  socat

CMD socat TCP-LISTEN:2375,reuseaddr,fork UNIX-CLIENT:/var/run/docker.sock 
```

I think this is a combination of two problems.

Problem 1: You can type a partial component name and get back logs.  I'm still poking around in the code, but obviously somewhere along the line, a unit is considered a match for the target if the target is a substring of or regex match for the unit name.

Problem 2: You're allowed to specify multiple targets for the `journal` subcommand.  This seems to me that it's, in part, copy/paste error.  Someone probably modeled the command and function signature after existing commands and functions that legitimately can take multiple targets (e.g. `start`).  After all, what does it mean to look at the logs for _two or more_ targets?

With the two problems combined, "controller" is a match for two units, and you're shown the logs for one, and then the other.

IMO, both of these should be fixed so that you can view logs for one target at a time and the name of the target needs to be fully articulated-- no fuzzy matches, else it gets us into trouble as it did here.

> from what i saw, you used the following techniques to speed up calculations

> * cache results by making it apply from aggregate (symmetry_looking)
> * do not sort parameters, use dict instead (ar_coefficient, ctw coefficients)
> * ?? (number_peaks)

One of the major things for increasing the performance was the reduction of the usage of pandas dataframes and series. They are very handle, but unfortunately very slow - especially when it comes to appending or concationating of results. Here, it is much better to store all results in a python list/dict and create a Series/DataFrame when everything is finished. Also, applying mean/std etc. and indexing is much faster on pure numpy arrays than on pandas Series.
Another optimization was to not use DataFrames for the configurations (e.g. in cwt_coefficients), because we would need to groupby/index/mask many times which is slow. Better use a cache for the calculated values and work through all configuration parameters one-by-one.

> I rebased to get the buildTransaction() changes, and did a forced push. Sorry if that makes the review confusing.

No worries, rebase seems like the best option you had.

> Do I need to support `functions` and `events` on the contract type invocation?

Yes.

> but then im not sure how to get all of the parameters I need, since at this point abi, address, web3 are absent from cls. . @pipermerriam Suggested removing the contract parameter from `ContractMethod()`, which I think is feasible, by moving `contract._find_matching_fn_abi` and `contract._prepare_transaction` to `ContractMethod`. Then I could just pass in abi, web3 and address from the factory kwargs.

Passing in abi and web3 here seems promising.

> Another problem I see is wouldnt know what to do if 'address' is absent from contract.factory kwargs.

Note that `address` is not available in the factory in any normal usage, because it's only supplied on initialization in `w3.eth.contract`: https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/web3/eth.py#L296-L301

On a static call to a function the `to` field will always have to be supplied in the action, like `.transact({'to': '0x....'})`. (It should also handle ENS names)

So the question is how to initialize `functions` in `factory()` but then have `functions` discover the address of the initialized contract later on an instance-call. One way to go is to initialize it once in the factory without an address (for static function calls), and then re-initialize it in `__init__` once `address` becomes available.

There are a lot of reasons that your transaction might hit an error condition. Figuring out why your transaction is reverting is simplest to do by reading the source of the contract.

Since the problem doesn't appear to be specific to Web3.py, I'm closing the issue. But feel free to continue discussing here. One alternative to consider is https://ethereum.stackexchange.com

You will get better & faster answers if your question has a minimal, complete, and verifiable example (in this case, that means including the contract source): https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve

One benefit of building an MCVE is that during the process, the answer often becomes clear without needing to post the question at all.

[the specs for the 'Big Iron' machine can be seen [here](https://python.g-node.org/python-summerschool-2014/starving_cpu)]

My prediction is that compression will beat non-compression in more and more scenarios as time goes by.

In hmmlearn, you feed a list of arrays to `fit`: a 3-d structure. That doesn't work with scikit-learn transformers unless you jump through hoops to wrap a 2-d array in this kind of structure. Similarly, `predict` always takes a single sequence rather than a sequence of sequences, making evaluation harder.

In seqlearn, I decided to stick to the 2-d `X` and 1-d `y` convention to denote a set of samples. The only difference with scikit-learn is that the order matters (but transformers will always respect the ordering) and that sequence start/end points are given in an optional extra argument. In theory, a seqlearn classifier can fit right into a `Pipeline`.

`TCPSite` works with IPv4/IPv6 addresses, `UnixSite` operates with Unix sockets.
The address format is different. Moreover, one *site* can have several sockets for dual IPv4/IPv6 stack.
I think we need a simple but robust solution that covers all cases.
Exposing `AbstractServer` is not the best idea may be: it opens `close()` method etc.

Perhaps the best approach is adding `addresses` property to *site* object which exposes a list of socket addresses in the same form as returned by `socket.getsockname()`.

1. Add `.host` and `.port` to `TCPSite`, `.path` for `UnixSite`.

3. Support the only `.address` property by all

> Oks, once the pipelining has been simplified using a naive implementation we can move forward to the next steps. @fafhrd91 you suggested that we might get rid of the StreamWriter once we have removed the complexity under the hood, my suggestion heads to remove the big fish, the PayloadWriter.
> 
> Right now Aiohttp needs at least the following three layers to handle the response an instance of StreamWriter, an instance of PayloadWriter and finally an instance of StreamResponse, also derivates of the last two ones are allowed. The idea is to reduce these three layers to just the StreamResponse one. My gut feeling says that it's doable that would be done making the following changes:
> 
> Get rid of the superfluous StreamWriter and turn the TCP tunning operations as simple functions.

```
from random import randint

import asyncio
import ssl

import aiomysql


async def main(loop):
    ctx = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1)
    ctx.check_hostname = False
    # ctx.load_verify_locations(cafile=' .. ')

    pool = None
    counter = 0

    while True:
        if not pool:
            pool = await aiomysql.create_pool(
                host=' ',
                port=33XX,
                user=' ',
                password=' ',
                loop=loop, ssl=ctx
            )

        asyncio.ensure_future(tls_query(counter, pool))

        counter += 1

        if not counter % randint(1, 4):
            print("Closing the pool")
            pool.close()
            await pool.wait_closed()
            pool = None

            if counter > 30:
                break
        elif not counter % 5:
            await asyncio.sleep(randint(0, 5))
        else:
            await asyncio.sleep(0)


async def tls_query(counter, pool):
    print(f"Running query {counter}")
    try:
        async with pool.get() as conn:
            async with conn.cursor() as cur:
                await cur.execute("select 1")
                value = await cur.fetchall()
                print(f"{counter} - {value}")
    except Exception as error:
        print(f"Failed {counter} {error}")  # Cannot acquire connection after closing pool


event_loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
event_loop.run_until_complete(main(event_loop))
pending = asyncio.Task.all_tasks()
event_loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(*pending))

print("Exiting")
```

In general, I agree with @takluyver that tags were created with the specific purpose of providing a single UI for defining labelling *and behavior* without having to create new, dedicated UI for every flag, which is required for any other metadata structure we come up with. As of now, Schematics offers no way to distinguish an explicit `None` from "not set" (or "missing"). This causes all kinds of headaches when dealing with partial data, as you've discovered.

I've wanted to fix this for a long time, but work on the feature was halted due to lack of feedback and other issues. But it's coming in the next few weeks.

Right now, I'm planning to deprecate `serialize_when_none` and replace it with a setting like `export_level`, which might look something like this:

  * 0: never export
  * 1: don't export when empty (`None`, `[]`, `{}`, undefined)
  * 2: don't export when `None` or undefined
  * 3: don't export when undefined
  * 4: always export

Levels 1 and 4 are what's currently being offered via `serialize_when_none`.

Also see #360, which deals with applying defaults to incoming data.

@GabrielFortin options are automatically supported once the resource itself is part of the API.

So, for the collection "HTTPs" (plural, NOT secure HTTP) there is this

https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/blob/development/f5/bigip/tm/ltm/profile.py#L441

And for the resources in that collection, there is the HTTP resource here

https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/blob/development/f5/bigip/tm/ltm/profile.py#L450

So, by convention, this means that you automatically already have access to all the mentioned options you're looking for.

Here is the payload for enforcement

```
      "enforcement": {
        "excessClientHeaders": "reject",
        "excessServerHeaders": "reject",
        "knownMethods": [
          "CONNECT",
          "DELETE",
          "GET",
          "HEAD",
          "LOCK",
          "OPTIONS",
          "POST",
          "PROPFIND",
          "PUT",
          "TRACE",
          "UNLOCK"
        ],
        "maxHeaderCount": 64,
        "maxHeaderSize": 32768,
        "maxRequests": 0,
        "oversizeClientHeaders": "reject",
        "oversizeServerHeaders": "reject",
        "pipeline": "allow",
        "truncatedRedirects": "disabled",
        "unknownMethod": "allow"
      },
```

which can be represented as a python `dict` like so

`profile.https.http.create(enforcement=dict(maxHeaderCount=100))`

Here is the payload for explicit-proxy

```
      "explicitProxy": {
        "defaultConnectHandling": "deny"
      },
```

which can be represented as a python `dict` also like so

`profile.https.http.create(explicitProxy=dict(defaultConnectHandling="deny"))

Here is the payload for sflow.

```
      "sflow": {
        "pollInterval": 0,
        "pollIntervalGlobal": "yes",
        "samplingRate": 0,
        "samplingRateGlobal": "yes"
      },
```

which can be represented as a python `dict` also like so

`profile.https.http.create(sflow=dict(pollInterval=0))

I think that given that the idea of `fuzzy` is to indicate that some parts of the string are unnecessary for specifying the date, ignoring the redundancy is a proper use of the  keyword.

I've pushed https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/commit/24854f4d22e1df3c0eaa816f4d7bbcde52a8b279 to demonstrate that the new test fails in non-UTC timezones (except for a few runs that failed to download the timezone database).

I followed that with https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/commit/9b57f676d8e579586269ba4fd55b635b42badba7 which shows that the fix works with the new tests (again, ignoring the cases where the update of the timezone database failed).

I think the reason here is that after the last transition, `dateutil` falls back to STD time, `pytz` evidently falls back to whatever the last transition was. In the southern hemisphere, the last 32-bit dates are zones are on the DST side, which creates the discrepancy.

I'm not sure there's a "right" answer here, but my inclination is that falling back to standard feels right.

The W3C recommendation, that I mensioned before assumes that date string consist only of a date without any date non-related characters. In this case there is no ambiguity. So this format could be only supported for non-fuzzy parsing.

pytz internally uses fixed offset timezones, swapping in the 'correct' one after arithmetic is completed, it is able to reliably do timeline arithmetic across DST boundaries. Arithmetic is done using the starting point's timezone rules (so DST in the original example). Adding one month will add one month in DST. When this is 'normalized' (in this case, implicitly by astimezone), the timezone is corrected to STD and you lose an hour.

I think the best way of mixing rrule and pytz is to strip the tzinfo using .replace(tzinfo=None), add the rrule, and then timezone.localize(result) to put the timezone back.

import pytz
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil.rrule import rrulestr

BERLIN = pytz.timezone('Europe/Berlin')
naive_local_time = datetime(2015, 4, 8, 19)
date_list = list(rrulestr('FREQ=MONTHLY;COUNT=12', dtstart=naive_local_time))
# translating these times to utc and back to berlin makes the error obvious
print([BERLIN.localize(dtime) for dtime in date_list])

The failure in Python 2 is due to an existing bug in pytest: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/2917 . I was able to work around it by upgrading pytest to the latest version and using `pytest.deprecated_call()`:

```python
def test_strict_is_deprecated():
    with pytest.deprecated_call():
        class StrictUserSchema(Schema):
            name = fields.String()

            class Meta:
                strict = False

    class UserSchema(Schema):
        name = fields.String()

    with pytest.deprecated_call():
        UserSchema(strict=True)
```

In the spirit of retaining the low-level, mostly non-object-oriented nature of pvlib, I think that we should could expand the definition of ``system_def`` to be "a general place to put crap about your PV system". If you use key words from the pvlib name list, then it will work when you ``**system_def`` into pvlib functions, but otherwise you can use the dict however you want. We could reduce the complexity of a lot of user code by allowing most pvlib methods to take ``**kwargs`` for the unused parts of the ``system_def`` dict. On a related note, dictionary unpacking got a lot more useful in Python 3.5 with the addition of more flexible unpacking tools ([PEP 448](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0448/)), so you can do things like 

``pvlib.some_method(**{**system_def, **my_other_params})``

I now think that methods such as ``get_solarposition`` should only take lat, lon, and tz arguments instead of a ``Location`` object. Furthermore, the ``Location`` objects would have a function that calls the ``get_solarposition`` method with the appropriate arguments based on the object attributes. So you could accomplish the same thing in two ways:

```python
site = pvlib.location.Location(lat, lon, tz, elev, name)
solpos = site.get_solarposition(times)
```

or

```python
site = {'lat':lat, 'lon':lon, 'tz':tz, 'elev':elev, 'name':name}
solpos = pvlib.solarposition.get_solarposition(**site)
```

To make the object oriented stuff work using the procedural code we can do things like

```python
class Location(object):
    # stuff like init

    def get_solarposition(self, times):
        return pvlib.solarposition.get_solarposition(times, self.lat, self.lon)

    # maybe other wrapper stuff for other module functions


class PVSystem(Location):
    def __init__(module_type, other_system_crap, args_and_kwargs_for_Location):
        self.module_type = module_type 
        # and so on for the rest of other_system_crap
        # maybe do something to make non-Location systems (i.e. indoor testing) reasonable
        super().__init__()

    def calcparams_desoto(self, poa_globa, temp_cell):
         return pvsystem.calcparams_desoto(poa_global, temp_cell, module_parameters=self.module_type)
    
    def singlediode(self, params)
        return pvsystem.singlediode(attributes, params)

    # remember that get_solarposition was inherited from Location, too
```

I worry about the maintenance cost for this, but maybe it would be worth it. @bmu objected to this inheritance, so that needs to be readdressed too.

I also found [this post](http://matthewrocklin.com/blog/work/2013/09/02/Shared-Abstractions/) by Matthew Rocklin to be helpful in thinking about dictionaries vs. classes.

RRTM is used widely in forecasting/climate models. It's a correlated-k model (see [this paper](http://irina.eas.gatech.edu/EAS8803_Fall2007/Fu_Liou1992.pdf) for example), which is calibrated using a line-by-line model that has been validated with observations. As far as I'm aware it is very accurate. I used the model for some fairly theoretical work with no clouds or aerosols, but the model does support these and is very comprehensive.

The python wrapper I wrote (which is pyrrtm as mentioned above) might be a useful starting point for someone who wanted to call the model from python. However, I did write it with my more theoretical use in mind so doesn't currently support clouds or aerosols. Adding these would probably be quite straightforward.

The problem arises when `electron-builder` tries to rebuild `zeromq`.

To rebuild it runs 
```
npm run install -- --disturl=https://atom.io/download/electron --target=1.4.6 --runtime=electron --arch=x64 --build-from-source
```
since zeromq uses [`prebuild`](https://github.com/mafintosh/prebuild) this will be expanded to
```
prebuild --install --preinstall "npm run build:libzmq" "--disturl=https://atom.io/download/electron" "--target=1.4.6" "--runtime=electron" "--arch=x64" "--build-from-source"
```

The problem is `target` is a option for [`prebuilt`](https://github.com/mafintosh/prebuild#help) defining the Node.js version to build against. Hence we get a download error because there is now Node.js version 1.4.6.

@develar Would it be better to use
```
npm rebuild --disturl=https://atom.io/download/electron --target=1.4.6 --runtime=electron --arch=x64 --build-from-source
```
instead of `npm run install` for rebuilding?

OK, this is a largely working PR which addresses mainly the bug in creating many GangaObject files in series as well as addressing some performance issues to do with memory.
In this PR I see Ganga able to construct many GangaObject class instances with RAM consumed at ~20Mb/s. The overall footprint in Ganga remains un-effected but the shutdown appears to be slightly faster from some tests due to the use of `__slots__`.
Within this PR I am able to construct 10k DiracFile objects within ~7sec where as on develop this can take ~30+sec. I believe the gains here are enough to push for inclusion of the branch as also loading a job with ~1000subjobs only takes 10sec on this branch vs 30-40sec on develop.

The most significant changes have been some minor changes to the constructor within the Proxy tool which was previously fully-constructing then modifying an underlying object instance. As the `__set__` method on GangaObject is known to be extremely heavy this turns out to be rather costly so I've changed it to construct an empty class and then populate it on construction.

The tests are largely uneffected by this but I plan to add a test to check that this doesn't get re-introduced.

In the interest of full disclosure this PR has turned up some minor bugs elsewhere which have been very quick to fix. This is because the order in which attributes are assigned has varied subtly from before. There isn't anything I see too alarming here but it's possible this PR could expose bugs to communities other than Core/LHCb that I simply don't know about.

I plan to go through and document a lot of the changes (probably next week due to other work) but I'd welcome input at this stage. I would like to push for these changes to be merged for 6.5 or 6.6 whichever is best.

Hi Logan,

Thank for your reply! 

for 1, I think adding an additional method to the NearNeighbor classes to get 2nd-nn is a good way to go. I am only a bit concerned about the performance issue, as it may includes some repeated calculation of 1st-nn in real applications. eg., if we want to get 2nd-nn of each atom in the structure and we already calculated the 1st-nn of all atoms (which is a more common case I think), we can just combine the 1st-nn of the 1st-nn atoms of each atom to get the 2nd-nn atoms, without further calculation. This requires solving the caching issues. But still, I think adding a standard way to get 2nd-nn is quite useful.

Besides, I have considered to add a new site-featurizer to allow "coarse-graining" (eg. getting 'mean', 'std_dev', 'minimum' or 'maximum') any site fingerprint around the neighbors to serve as site features. This featurizer could also takes the 2nd-NN neighboring effect into account. The user can give the featurizer a type of site fingerprint and a neighbor finding algorithm (including Voronoi method) and the featurizer will return a coarse grained version of that site fingerprint. This works similarly as the SiteStatsFingerprint, except for serving as site features rather than structure features. But I am also quite concerned about the performance issue, especially if the neighbor finding is quite computationally expensive, as you have mentioned. I have also tested some matminer featurizers on the disordered systems that contain tens/hundreds of thousands of atoms and found that it indeed takes quite a long time to featurize. 

for 2 and 3 (and 1), I totally agree that it will be great if we can also share results among featurizers, in addition to within a single featurizer. This will solve many issues and save a lot of computation time. Some steps, e.g., getting atoms within a cutoff, getting neighborlist etc, are indeed frequently called by a number of featurizers. I think perhaps methods like `joblib.Memory` (for caching a function's return values each time it is called with the same input arguments, also used in `sklearn`) might be useful? or even create some tmp db files in a standard way (might be less preferable). How do you feel about this?

A duplicate transaction could mean a few things:

1. A nonce is reused
2. The transaction for the same side-effect is used twice

The first case is considered an usage error. It's assumed that Raiden is the only application controlling the account, if there are two transactions with the same nonce either another app is using the same account, which is not supported (#1400), there is a bug in Raiden which should crash the node, or a pending transaction was removed from the ethereum node poll, which is an extreme corner case which we are not handling ATM.

The second case was handled by #2168

As we can only give one sample split to TPOT at the beginning of the optimization process, the pipeline seems to overfit this special split during fitting, even if there are many splits. 
In the end, this results in a good score reported by TPOT, but when evaluating the best pipeline on other splits, the performance is much worse. I think a simple reproducing example could be built, if you wanted, I could give it a try.

There may be two options to overcome this: 
1. Changing the random_state at each iteration as suggested by CSNoyes
2. Randomizing splits at each iteration. 

To me, option 1 seems more easy. As an additional option to TPOT, it wouldn't hurt the overall performance of TPOT.

class ToPILImage(object):
    """Converts a torch.*Tensor of range [0, 1] and shape C x H x W
    or numpy ndarray of dtype=uint8, range[0, 255] and shape H x W x C
    to a PIL.Image of range [0, 255]
    """

    def __call__(self, pic):
        npimg = pic
        mode = None
        if not isinstance(npimg, np.ndarray):
            npimg = pic.mul(255).byte().numpy()
            npimg = np.transpose(npimg, (1, 2, 0))

        if npimg.shape[2] == 1:
            npimg = npimg[:, :, 0]

@stephanie-wang @robertnishihara I want to add some color here. #1128 is an _extremely subtle and serious performance bug_ that is very hard for new (or even experienced) users to debug. It commonly manifests itself in a Ray cluster behaving as if it only has a single node.

It's quite easy to hit; in fact, RLlib has completely stopped using idiomatic `ray.wait` because of this problem.

That said, there a probably a few ways to solve it while preserving semantics. A couple acceptable ways of prioritizing returns would be:
1. random sampling
2. the order the user passed the ids in (if it's clear in the documentation)
3. the order the objects were sealed in

Unacceptable ways of prioritizing would be:
1. whatever order the object manager happened to return them in

@dsariel You're making assumptions real quick. I didn't imply you should run `rdopkg -l patch` followed by `rdopkg patch` in any way. I only ran `-l` to test locally, it's completely the same as introducing the patch into patches branch and running without `-l' including same exit codes. 

Similarly, you assumed that "While the same patch is applied to rhos9 branch, everything is ok." but that wasn't the case, the patch wasn't applied and rdopkg was pretty clear it wasn't applied and `git show` would tell you the same. `rdopkg` returned 0 because the patches (already present in distgit) changed in patches branch. If you had clean patches branch, `rdopkg` would fail with 'No new patches' as it did for `rhos-8`.

You run `rdopkg patch` OR `rdopkg -l patch` depending on what you want to do and then inspect output. The point is to always run a single command for a single operation. I see no problem with that or how rdopkg is used, I only see problem of people not being able to easily tell whether the run was successfull or not.

That's mainly because running `rdopkg patch` while there are no new patches returns non-zero exit code which made sense for some interactive and scripting use cases, but you don't seem to care whether there were new patches, only whether the patching process was successful or not if I understand correctly.
